question_html: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} are such that {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex}, then find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} are such that {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} , then find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} Now {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k + \lambda \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} = {tex} = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k - \lambda \hat i + 2\lambda \hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a + \lambda \vec b = \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex} Again, {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Since, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right){/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} therefore, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 - 3\lambda + 2 + 2\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow - \lambda + 8 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - \lambda = - 8 \Rightarrow \lambda = 8{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} Now {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k + \lambda \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} = {tex} = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k - \lambda \hat i + 2\lambda \hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a + \lambda \vec b = \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex} Again, {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Since, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right){/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} therefore, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 - 3\lambda + 2 + 2\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow - \lambda + 8 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - \lambda = - 8 \Rightarrow \lambda = 8{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} 

Now {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k + \lambda \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}=

{tex} = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k - \lambda \hat i + 2\lambda \hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \vec a + \lambda \vec b = \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex}

Again, {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

Since, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right){/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} therefore, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 6 - 3\lambda + 2 + 2\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow - \lambda + 8 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow - \lambda = - 8 \Rightarrow \lambda = 8{/tex}

Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar.
Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex}...(i)
Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec d = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {160\hat i - 5\hat j - 70\hat k} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\lambda {/tex} and {tex}\mu {/tex} if {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\lambda {/tex} and {tex}\mu {/tex} if {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&6&{27} \\ 1&\lambda &u \end{array}} \right| = \vec 0{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \vec 0 = 0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides, we have {tex}6\mu - 27\lambda = 0{/tex} …(i) {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0{/tex} …(ii) And {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0{/tex} ...(iii) From eq. (ii), {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} From eq. (iii), {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex} Putting the values of {tex}\mu {/tex} and {tex}\lambda {/tex} in eq. (i), {tex}6\left( {\frac{{27}}{2}} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {27} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 =0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mu = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda = 3.{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&6&{27} \\ 1&\lambda &u \end{array}} \right| = \vec 0{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \vec 0 = 0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides, we have {tex}6\mu - 27\lambda = 0{/tex} …(i) {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0{/tex} …(ii) And {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0{/tex} ...(iii) From eq. (ii), {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} From eq. (iii), {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex} Putting the values of {tex}\mu {/tex} and {tex}\lambda {/tex} in eq. (i), {tex}6\left( {\frac{{27}}{2}} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {27} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 =0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mu = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda = 3.{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&6&{27} \\ 1&\lambda &u \end{array}} \right| = \vec 0{/tex}

Expanding along first row,

{tex}\hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \vec 0 = 0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides, we have

{tex}6\mu - 27\lambda = 0{/tex} …(i)

{tex}2\mu - 27 = 0{/tex} …(ii)

And {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0{/tex} ...(iii)

From eq. (ii), {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex}

From eq. (iii), {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex}

Putting the values of {tex}\mu {/tex} and {tex}\lambda {/tex} in eq. (i),

{tex}6\left( {\frac{{27}}{2}} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {27} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 =0{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\mu = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda = 3.{/tex}

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Renu purchased an air plant holder which is in the shape of a tetrahedron.
Let A, B, C and D are the coordinates of the air plant holder where A {tex}\equiv{/tex} (1, 1, 1), B {tex}\equiv{/tex} (2, 1, 3), C {tex}\equiv{/tex} (3, 2, 2) and D {tex}\equiv{/tex} (3, 3, 4).

  1. Find the position vector of {tex}\vec{AB}{/tex} (1)
  2. Find the position vector of {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC (2)
    OR
    Find the unit vector along {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex} (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Renu purchased an air plant holder which is in the shape of a tetrahedron. Let A, B, C and D are the coordinates of the air plant holder where A {tex}\equiv{/tex} (1, 1, 1), B {tex}\equiv{/tex} (2, 1, 3), C {tex}\equiv{/tex} (3, 2, 2) and D {tex}\equiv{/tex} (3, 3, 4). Find the position vector of {tex}\vec{AB}{/tex} (1) Find the position vector of {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex} . (1) Find area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC (2) OR Find the unit vector along {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex} (2)
explanation: Position vector of {tex}\vec{AB}{/tex} = (2-1) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (1-1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3-1) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} Position vector of {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex} = (3 - 1) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (3 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} +(4 - 1) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex} {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} (0-2) - {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} (1-4) + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} (1-0) = -2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A B} \times \vec {A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+1}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. units OR Unit vector along {tex}\vec {A D}=\frac{\vec{A D}}{|\vec{A D}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k}{\sqrt{2^{2}+2^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+4+9}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}}{/tex} (2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} )
solution: Position vector of {tex}\vec{AB}{/tex} = (2-1) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (1-1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3-1) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} Position vector of {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex} = (3 - 1) {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (3 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} +(4 - 1) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex} {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} (0-2) - {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} (1-4) + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} (1-0) = -2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A B} \times \vec {A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+1}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. units OR Unit vector along {tex}\vec {A D}=\frac{\vec{A D}}{|\vec{A D}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k}{\sqrt{2^{2}+2^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+4+9}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}}{/tex} (2 {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} )
solution_html:
1. Position vector of {tex}\vec{AB}{/tex}
    = (2-1){tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (1-1){tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3-1){tex}\hat k{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat{k}{/tex}

  2. Position vector of {tex}\vec{AD}{/tex}
    = (3 - 1){tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + (3 - 1){tex}\hat j{/tex} +(4 - 1){tex}\hat k{/tex} = 2{tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat k{/tex}

  3. Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex}(0-2) - {tex}\hat{j}{/tex}(1-4) + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex}(1-0)
    = -2{tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + {tex}\hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A B} \times \vec {A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+1}=\sqrt{14}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{14}{/tex} sq. units
    OR
    Unit vector along {tex}\vec {A D}=\frac{\vec{A D}}{|\vec{A D}|}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 k}{\sqrt{2^{2}+2^{2}+3^{2}}}=\frac{2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+4+9}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}}{/tex} (2{tex}\hat{i}{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat{j}{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat{k}{/tex})

explanation: {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=-2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} We have, A(1, 4, 2), B(3, -3, -2) and C(-2, 2, 6) Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {A C}=\vec{c}-\vec{a}=-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -7 & -4 \\ -3 & -2 & 4\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} (-28 - 8) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (8 - 12) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (-4 - 21) = -36 {tex}\hat i{/tex} +4 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 25 {tex}\hat k{/tex} Now, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}| =\sqrt{(-36)^{2}+4^{2}+(-25)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1296+16+625}=\sqrt{1937}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{A B} \times \overrightarrow{A C}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{1937}{/tex} sq. units If the given points lie on the straight line, then the points will be collinear and so area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{b} \times \vec{c}+\bar{c} \times \vec{a}|{/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} If {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} are the position vectors of the three vertices A, B and C of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC, then area of triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{b} \times \vec{c}+\vec{c} \times \vec{a}|{/tex} ] OR Here, {tex}\overrightarrow a=2\widehat i+3\widehat j+6\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\left|a\right|}=\sqrt{4+9+36}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex} Now unit vector {tex}\widehat a=\frac{2\widehat i+3\widehat j+6\widehat k}7{/tex} {tex}\widehat a=\frac27\widehat i+\frac37\widehat j+\frac67\widehat k{/tex}

    {tex}\therefore \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}

    Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}-\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {A C}=\vec{c}-\vec{a}=-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Girish left from his village on weekend. First, he travelled up to temple. After this, he left for the zoo. After this he left for shopping in a mall. The positions of Girish at different places is given in the following graph. Find position vector of B (1) Find position vector of D (1) Find the vector {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} in terms of {tex}\hat i{/tex} , {tex}\hat j{/tex} . (2) OR Find the length of vector {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: Here (5, 3) are the coordinates of B. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of B = 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Here (9, 8) are the coordinates of D. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of D = 9 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of B = 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} and Position vecto of C = 6 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 5 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} = (6 - 5) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 3) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} OR Since P.V. of A = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} , P.V. of D = 9 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = (9 - 2) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (8 - 2) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = 7 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} | {tex}\overrightarrow {AD}{/tex} | 2 = 7 2 + 6 2 = 49 + 36 = 85 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} | = {tex}\sqrt {85}{/tex} units
solution: Here (5, 3) are the coordinates of B. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of B = 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Here (9, 8) are the coordinates of D. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of D = 9 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of B = 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} and Position vecto of C = 6 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 5 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} = (6 - 5) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 3) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} OR Since P.V. of A = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2 {tex}\hat j{/tex} , P.V. of D = 9 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = (9 - 2) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (8 - 2) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = 7 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} | {tex}\overrightarrow {AD}{/tex} | 2 = 7 2 + 6 2 = 49 + 36 = 85 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} | {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} | = {tex}\sqrt {85}{/tex} units
solution_html:
1. Here (5, 3) are the coordinates of B.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of B = 5{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat j{/tex}

  2. Here (9, 8) are the coordinates of D.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of D = 9{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8{tex}\hat j{/tex}

  3. Position vector of B = 5{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} and Position vecto of C = 6{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 5{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} = (6 - 5){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (5 - 3){tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    OR
    Since P.V. of A = 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2{tex}\hat j{/tex}, P.V. of D = 9{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 8{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} = (9 - 2){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (8 - 2){tex}\hat j{/tex} = 7{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    |{tex}\overrightarrow {AD}{/tex}|2 = 72 + 62 = 49 + 36 = 85
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |{tex}\vec {AD}{/tex}| = {tex}\sqrt {85}{/tex} units

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A building is to be constructed in the form of a triangular pyramid, ABCD as shown in the figure.

Let its angular points are A(0, 1, 2), B(3, 0, 1), C(4, 3, 6) and D(2, 3, 2) and G be the point of intersection of the medians of {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD.

  1. Find the coordinates of point G (1)
  2. Find the length of vector {tex}\vec {AG}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC (in sq. units). (2)
    OR
    Find the length of the perpendicular from the vertex D on the opposite face. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A building is to be constructed in the form of a triangular pyramid, ABCD as shown in the figure. Let its angular points are A(0, 1, 2), B(3, 0, 1), C(4, 3, 6) and D(2, 3, 2) and G be the point of intersection of the medians of {tex}\triangle{/tex} BCD. Find the coordinates of point G (1) Find the length of vector {tex}\vec {AG}{/tex} . (1) Find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC (in sq. units). (2) OR Find the length of the perpendicular from the vertex D on the opposite face. (2)
explanation: Clearly, G be the centroid of {tex}\triangle{/tex} BCD, therefore coordinates of G are {tex}\left(\frac{3+4+2}{3}, \frac{0+3+3}{3}, \frac{1+6+2}{3}\right){/tex} = (3, 2, 3) Since, A {tex}\equiv{/tex} (0, 1, 2) and G = (3, 2, 3) {tex}\therefore \vec {A G}{/tex} = (3 - 0) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (2 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}|^{2}{/tex} = 3 2 + 1 2 + 1 2 = 9 + 1 + 1 = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}| = \sqrt {11}{/tex} Clearly, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3-0 & 0-1 & 1-2 \\ 4-0 & 3-1 & 6-2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -1 & -1 \\ 4 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (-4 + 2) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (12 + 4) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (6 + 4) = -2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 16 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+256+100}=\sqrt{360}=6 \sqrt{10}{/tex} Hence, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \sqrt{10}=3 \sqrt{10}{/tex} sq. units OR The length of the perpendicular from the vertex D on the opposite face = |Projection of {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} on {tex}\vec {AB} \times \vec {AC}{/tex} | = {tex}\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-4-32}{\sqrt{360}}\right|=\frac{36}{6 \sqrt{10}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{10}}{/tex} units
solution: Clearly, G be the centroid of {tex}\triangle{/tex} BCD, therefore coordinates of G are {tex}\left(\frac{3+4+2}{3}, \frac{0+3+3}{3}, \frac{1+6+2}{3}\right){/tex} = (3, 2, 3) Since, A {tex}\equiv{/tex} (0, 1, 2) and G = (3, 2, 3) {tex}\therefore \vec {A G}{/tex} = (3 - 0) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (2 - 1) {tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3 - 2) {tex}\hat k{/tex} = 3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}|^{2}{/tex} = 3 2 + 1 2 + 1 2 = 9 + 1 + 1 = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}| = \sqrt {11}{/tex} Clearly, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3-0 & 0-1 & 1-2 \\ 4-0 & 3-1 & 6-2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -1 & -1 \\ 4 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (-4 + 2) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (12 + 4) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (6 + 4) = -2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 16 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 10 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+256+100}=\sqrt{360}=6 \sqrt{10}{/tex} Hence, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \sqrt{10}=3 \sqrt{10}{/tex} sq. units OR The length of the perpendicular from the vertex D on the opposite face = |Projection of {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} on {tex}\vec {AB} \times \vec {AC}{/tex} | = {tex}\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-4-32}{\sqrt{360}}\right|=\frac{36}{6 \sqrt{10}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{10}}{/tex} units
solution_html:
1. Clearly, G be the centroid of {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD, therefore coordinates of G are
    {tex}\left(\frac{3+4+2}{3}, \frac{0+3+3}{3}, \frac{1+6+2}{3}\right){/tex} = (3, 2, 3)

  2. Since, A {tex}\equiv{/tex} (0, 1, 2) and G = (3, 2, 3)
    {tex}\therefore \vec {A G}{/tex} = (3 - 0){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (2 - 1){tex}\hat j{/tex} + (3 - 2){tex}\hat k{/tex} = 3{tex}\hat i{/tex} + {tex}\hat j{/tex} + {tex}\hat k{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}|^{2}{/tex} = 32 + 12 + 12 = 9 + 1 + 1 = 11
    {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec {A G}| = \sqrt {11}{/tex}

  3. Clearly, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|{/tex}
    Here, {tex}\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3-0 & 0-1 & 1-2 \\ 4-0 & 3-1 & 6-2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -1 & -1 \\ 4 & 2 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\hat i{/tex}(-4 + 2) - {tex}\hat j{/tex}(12 + 4) + {tex}\hat k{/tex}(6 + 4) = -2{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 16{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 10{tex}\hat k{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore |\vec{A B} \times \vec{A C}|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{4+256+100}=\sqrt{360}=6 \sqrt{10}{/tex}
    Hence, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \sqrt{10}=3 \sqrt{10}{/tex} sq. units
    OR
    The length of the perpendicular from the vertex D on the opposite face
    = |Projection of {tex}\vec {AD}{/tex} on {tex}\vec {AB} \times \vec {AC}{/tex}|
    = {tex}\left|\frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-16)^{2}+10^{2}}}\right|{/tex}
    = {tex}\left|\frac{-4-32}{\sqrt{360}}\right|=\frac{36}{6 \sqrt{10}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt{10}}{/tex} units

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A plane started from airport O with a velocity of 120 m/s towards east. Air is blowing at a velocity of 50 m/s towards the north As shown in the figure.
The plane travelled 1 hr in OA direction with the resultant velocity. From A and B travelled 1 hr with keeping velocity of 120 m/s and finally landed at B.

  1. What is the resultant velocity from O to A? (1)
  2. What is the direction of travel of plane O to A with east? (1)
  3. What is the total displacement from O to A? (2)
    OR
    What is the resultant velocity from A to B? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A plane started from airport O with a velocity of 120 m/s towards east. Air is blowing at a velocity of 50 m/s towards the north As shown in the figure. The plane travelled 1 hr in OA direction with the resultant velocity. From A and B travelled 1 hr with keeping velocity of 120 m/s and finally landed at B. What is the resultant velocity from O to A? (1) What is the direction of travel of plane O to A with east? (1) What is the total displacement from O to A? (2) OR What is the resultant velocity from A to B? (2)
explanation: Resultant velocity from O to A {tex}=\sqrt{\left(V_{\text {Plane }}\right)^2+\left(V_{\text {wind }}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(120)^2+(50)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14400+2500}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16900}{/tex} = 130 m/s {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{V_{\text {wind }}}{V_{\text {aeroplane }}}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{50}{120}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{5}{12}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right){/tex} Displacement from O to A = Resultant velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time {tex}|\overrightarrow{O A}|=|\vec{V}| \times t{/tex} {tex}=130 \times \frac{18}{5} \times 1{/tex} = 468 km OR Since, from A to B both Aeroplane and wind have velocity in North direction. So, {tex}\vec V _{plane, A to B}{/tex} = 120 + 50 = 170 m/s
solution: Resultant velocity from O to A {tex}=\sqrt{\left(V_{\text {Plane }}\right)^2+\left(V_{\text {wind }}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(120)^2+(50)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14400+2500}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16900}{/tex} = 130 m/s {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{V_{\text {wind }}}{V_{\text {aeroplane }}}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{50}{120}{/tex} {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{5}{12}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right){/tex} Displacement from O to A = Resultant velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time {tex}|\overrightarrow{O A}|=|\vec{V}| \times t{/tex} {tex}=130 \times \frac{18}{5} \times 1{/tex} = 468 km OR Since, from A to B both Aeroplane and wind have velocity in North direction. So, {tex}\vec V _{plane, A to B}{/tex} = 120 + 50 = 170 m/s
solution_html:
1. Resultant velocity from O to A
    {tex}=\sqrt{\left(V_{\text {Plane }}\right)^2+\left(V_{\text {wind }}\right)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{(120)^2+(50)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{14400+2500}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{16900}{/tex}
    = 130 m/s

  2. {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{V_{\text {wind }}}{V_{\text {aeroplane }}}{/tex}
    {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{50}{120}{/tex}
    {tex}\tan \theta=\frac{5}{12}{/tex}
    {tex}\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right){/tex}

  3. Displacement from O to A = Resultant velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time
    {tex}|\overrightarrow{O A}|=|\vec{V}| \times t{/tex}
    {tex}=130 \times \frac{18}{5} \times 1{/tex}
    = 468 km
    OR
    Since, from A to B both Aeroplane and wind have velocity in North direction.
    So,
    {tex}\vec V _{plane, A to B}{/tex} = 120 + 50
    = 170 m/s

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Ram's house is situated at Gandhi Nagar at Point O, for going to school he first travels by bus in the east. Here at Point A, a hospital is situated. From Hospital Ram takes an auto and goes 3 km in the north direction, here at point B school is situated. Suresh's house is at 30 o east, 6 km from point B. Find the displacement between Ram's house and school. (1) How many km Ram travels to reach school? (1) What is the vector distance from school to Suresh's home? (2) OR What is the displacement from Ram's house to Suresh house? (2)
explanation: Displacement between Ram's house and school = {tex}\sqrt {4^2+3^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {25}{/tex} = 5 km Distance travelled to reach school by Ram = 4 + 3 = 7 km Position vector of school = 4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of Suresh's = (4 + 6 cos30) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 + 6 sin30) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}(4 + \frac {6\sqrt 3}{2})\hat i + (3 + \frac {6 \times 1}{2})\hat j{/tex} = (4 + {tex}3 \sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Vector distance from school to Suresh's home [(4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} ] - [(4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} )] {tex}3 \sqrt{3} \hat i + 3 \hat j{/tex} OR Position vector of Ram's house = 0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of Suresh's house = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Displacement from Ram's house to suresh's house = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - (0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} ) {tex}(4+3 \sqrt{3}) \hat i + 6 \hat j{/tex}
solution: Displacement between Ram's house and school = {tex}\sqrt {4^2+3^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {25}{/tex} = 5 km Distance travelled to reach school by Ram = 4 + 3 = 7 km Position vector of school = 4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of Suresh's = (4 + 6 cos30) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 + 6 sin30) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}(4 + \frac {6\sqrt 3}{2})\hat i + (3 + \frac {6 \times 1}{2})\hat j{/tex} = (4 + {tex}3 \sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Vector distance from school to Suresh's home [(4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} ] - [(4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} )] {tex}3 \sqrt{3} \hat i + 3 \hat j{/tex} OR Position vector of Ram's house = 0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} Position vector of Suresh's house = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Displacement from Ram's house to suresh's house = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex} ) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - (0 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} ) {tex}(4+3 \sqrt{3}) \hat i + 6 \hat j{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Displacement between Ram's house and school = {tex}\sqrt {4^2+3^2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt {25}{/tex}
    = 5 km

  2. Distance travelled to reach school by Ram = 4 + 3
    = 7 km

  3. Position vector of school = 4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    Position vector of Suresh's
    = (4 + 6 cos30){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (3 + 6 sin30){tex}\hat j{/tex}
    = {tex}(4 + \frac {6\sqrt 3}{2})\hat i + (3 + \frac {6 \times 1}{2})\hat j{/tex}
    = (4 + {tex}3 \sqrt 3{/tex}){tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    Vector distance from school to Suresh's home
    [(4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex}){tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex}] - [(4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 3{tex}\hat j{/tex})]
    {tex}3 \sqrt{3} \hat i + 3 \hat j{/tex}
    OR
    Position vector of Ram's house = 0{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    Position vector of Suresh's house = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex}){tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Displacement from Ram's house to suresh's house
    = (4 + {tex}3\sqrt 3{/tex}){tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} - (0{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0{tex}\hat j{/tex})

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Once Ramesh was going to his native place at a village near Agra. From Delhi and Agra he went by flight, In the way, there was a river. Ramesh reached the river by taxi. Then Ramesh used a boat for crossing the river. The boat heads directly across the river 40 m wide at 4 m/s. The current was flowing downstream at 3 m/s.

  1. What is the resultant velocity of the boat? (1)
  2. How much time does it take the boat to cross the river? (1)
  3. How far downstream is the boat when it reaches the other side? (2)
    OR
    If speeds of boat and current were 1.5 m/s and 2.0 m/s then what will be resultant velocity? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Once Ramesh was going to his native place at a village near Agra. From Delhi and Agra he went by flight, In the way, there was a river. Ramesh reached the river by taxi. Then Ramesh used a boat for crossing the river. The boat heads directly across the river 40 m wide at 4 m/s. The current was flowing downstream at 3 m/s. What is the resultant velocity of the boat? (1) How much time does it take the boat to cross the river? (1) How far downstream is the boat when it reaches the other side? (2) OR If speeds of boat and current were 1.5 m/s and 2.0 m/s then what will be resultant velocity? (2)
explanation: Resultant velocity of boat {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 m/s Time taken by boat to cross the river {tex}=\frac{\text {Width of river}}{\text {Resultant velocity of boat}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{5}{/tex} = 8 sec Downstream distance travelled by boat = downstream speed {tex}\times{/tex} time taken by boat to cross the river = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 24 m OR Resultant velocity of boat {tex}=\sqrt{(1.5)^2+(2)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2.25 + 4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6.25}{/tex} = 2.5 m/sec
solution: Resultant velocity of boat {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 m/s Time taken by boat to cross the river {tex}=\frac{\text {Width of river}}{\text {Resultant velocity of boat}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{5}{/tex} = 8 sec Downstream distance travelled by boat = downstream speed {tex}\times{/tex} time taken by boat to cross the river = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 24 m OR Resultant velocity of boat {tex}=\sqrt{(1.5)^2+(2)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2.25 + 4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{6.25}{/tex} = 2.5 m/sec
solution_html:
1. Resultant velocity of boat
    {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+3^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{25}{/tex}
    = 5 m/s

  2. Time taken by boat to cross the river {tex}=\frac{\text {Width of river}}{\text {Resultant velocity of boat}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{40}{5}{/tex}
    = 8 sec

  3. Downstream distance travelled by boat = downstream speed {tex}\times{/tex} time taken by boat to cross the river
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 8
    = 24 m
    OR
    Resultant velocity of boat {tex}=\sqrt{(1.5)^2+(2)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{2.25 + 4}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{6.25}{/tex}
    = 2.5 m/sec

Three friends Ganesh, Dinesh and Ramesh went for playing a Tug of war game. Team A, B, and C belong to Ganesh, Dinesh and Ramesh respectively.
Teams A, B, C have attached a rope to a metal ring and is trying to pull the ring into their own area (team areas shown below).
Team A pulls with F1 = 4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0{tex}\hat j{/tex} KN
Team B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F2 = -2{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat j{/tex} KN
Team C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F3 = -3{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} KN

  1. Which team will win the game? (1)
  2. What is the magnitude of the teams combine Force? (1)
  3. What is the magnitude of the force of Team B? (2)
    OR

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Three friends Ganesh, Dinesh and Ramesh went for playing a Tug of war game. Team A, B, and C belong to Ganesh, Dinesh and Ramesh respectively. Teams A, B, C have attached a rope to a metal ring and is trying to pull the ring into their own area (team areas shown below). Team A pulls with F 1 = 4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} KN Team B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F 2 = -2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat j{/tex} KN Team C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} F 3 = -3 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} KN Which team will win the game? (1) What is the magnitude of the teams combine Force? (1) What is the magnitude of the force of Team B? (2) OR How many KN Force is applied by Team A? (2)
explanation: Force applied by team A = {tex}\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex} = 4 N Force applied by team B {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} Force applied by team C {tex}=\sqrt{(-3)^2+(-3)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Hence, the force applied by team B is maximum. So, Team 'B' will win. Sum of force applied by team A, B and C = (4 + (-2) + (-3)) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (0 + 4 + (-3)) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex} Magnitude of team combine force {tex}=\sqrt{(-1)^2+(1)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} N{/tex} Force applied by team B {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} OR Force applied by team A {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex} = 4 N
solution: Force applied by team A = {tex}\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex} = 4 N Force applied by team B {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} Force applied by team C {tex}=\sqrt{(-3)^2+(-3)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} Hence, the force applied by team B is maximum. So, Team 'B' will win. Sum of force applied by team A, B and C = (4 + (-2) + (-3)) {tex}\hat i{/tex} + (0 + 4 + (-3)) {tex}\hat j{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex} Magnitude of team combine force {tex}=\sqrt{(-1)^2+(1)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} N{/tex} Force applied by team B {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex} OR Force applied by team A {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex} = 4 N
solution_html:
1. Force applied by team A
    = {tex}\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex}
    = 4 N
    Force applied by team B
    {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex}
    {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}
    Force applied by team C
    {tex}=\sqrt{(-3)^2+(-3)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
    Hence, the force applied by team B is maximum.
    So, Team 'B' will win.

  2. Sum of force applied by team A, B and C
    = (4 + (-2) + (-3)){tex}\hat i{/tex} + (0 + 4 + (-3)){tex}\hat j{/tex}
    = {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex}
    Magnitude of team combine force
    {tex}=\sqrt{(-1)^2+(1)^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{2} N{/tex}

  3. Force applied by team B
    {tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+4^2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{4+16}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{20}{/tex}
    {tex}=2 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~N}{/tex}
    OR
    Force applied by team A
    {tex}=\sqrt{4^2+0^2}{/tex}
    = 4 N

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
If two vectors are represented by the two sides of a triangle taken in order, then their sum is represented by the third side of the triangle taken in opposite order and this is known as triangle law of vector addition.

  1. If {tex}\vec p, \vec q, \vec r{/tex} are the vectors represented by the sides of a triangle taken in order, then find {tex}\vec q + \vec r{/tex}. (1)
  2. If ABCD is a parallelogram and AC and BD are its diagonals, then find the value of {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} + \overrightarrow {BD}{/tex}. (1)
  3. If ABCD is a parallelogram, where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB}{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {BC}{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec b{/tex}, then find the value of {tex}\overrightarrow{AC} - \overrightarrow{BD}{/tex}. (2)
    OR
    If T is the mid point of side YZ of {tex}\triangle{/tex}XYZ, then what is the value of {tex}\vec{XY} + \vec {XZ}{/tex}. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: If two vectors are represented by the two sides of a triangle taken in order, then their sum is represented by the third side of the triangle taken in opposite order and this is known as triangle law of vector addition. If {tex}\vec p, \vec q, \vec r{/tex} are the vectors represented by the sides of a triangle taken in order, then find {tex}\vec q + \vec r{/tex} . (1) If ABCD is a parallelogram and AC and BD are its diagonals, then find the value of {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} + \overrightarrow {BD}{/tex} . (1) If ABCD is a parallelogram, where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {BC}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec b{/tex} , then find the value of {tex}\overrightarrow{AC} - \overrightarrow{BD}{/tex} . (2) OR If T is the mid point of side YZ of {tex}\triangle{/tex} XYZ, then what is the value of {tex}\vec{XY} + \vec {XZ}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: Let OAB be a triangle such that {tex}\vec{A O}=-\vec{p}, \vec{A B}=\vec{q}, \vec{B O}=\vec{r}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec q + \vec r = \vec{AB} + \vec{BO}{/tex} {tex}= \vec {AO} = - \vec p{/tex} From triangle law of vector addition, {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}+\overrightarrow{B D}=\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow {C D}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow {A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}-\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} {tex}[\because \overrightarrow{A B}=-\overrightarrow{C D}]{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC, {tex}\overrightarrow {AC}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + 2 {tex}\vec b{/tex} ...(i) and in {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABD, 2 {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\overrightarrow {BD}{/tex} ...(ii) [By triangle law of addition] Adding (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\overrightarrow {A C}+2 \vec{b}=4 \vec{a}+\overrightarrow {B D}+2 \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A C}-\overrightarrow{B D}=4 \vec{a}{/tex} OR Since T is the mid point of YZ So, {tex}\overrightarrow {YT} = \overrightarrow {TZ}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{X Y}+\overrightarrow{X Z}=(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y})+(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Z}){/tex} [By triangle law] = 2 {tex}\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y}+\overrightarrow{T Z}=2 \overrightarrow{X T}{/tex} [ {tex}\because \overrightarrow{TY} = -\overrightarrow {YT}{/tex} ]
solution: Let OAB be a triangle such that {tex}\vec{A O}=-\vec{p}, \vec{A B}=\vec{q}, \vec{B O}=\vec{r}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec q + \vec r = \vec{AB} + \vec{BO}{/tex} {tex}= \vec {AO} = - \vec p{/tex} From triangle law of vector addition, {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}+\overrightarrow{B D}=\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow {C D}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow {A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}-\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} {tex}[\because \overrightarrow{A B}=-\overrightarrow{C D}]{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC, {tex}\overrightarrow {AC}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + 2 {tex}\vec b{/tex} ...(i) and in {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABD, 2 {tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\overrightarrow {BD}{/tex} ...(ii) [By triangle law of addition] Adding (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\overrightarrow {A C}+2 \vec{b}=4 \vec{a}+\overrightarrow {B D}+2 \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A C}-\overrightarrow{B D}=4 \vec{a}{/tex} OR Since T is the mid point of YZ So, {tex}\overrightarrow {YT} = \overrightarrow {TZ}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{X Y}+\overrightarrow{X Z}=(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y})+(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Z}){/tex} [By triangle law] = 2 {tex}\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y}+\overrightarrow{T Z}=2 \overrightarrow{X T}{/tex} [ {tex}\because \overrightarrow{TY} = -\overrightarrow {YT}{/tex} ]
solution_html:
1. Let OAB be a triangle such that

    {tex}\vec{A O}=-\vec{p}, \vec{A B}=\vec{q}, \vec{B O}=\vec{r}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\vec q + \vec r = \vec{AB} + \vec{BO}{/tex}
    {tex}= \vec {AO} = - \vec p{/tex}

  2. From triangle law of vector addition,
    {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}+\overrightarrow{B D}=\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex}

    = {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow {C D}{/tex}
    = {tex}\overrightarrow {A B}+2 \overrightarrow{B C}-\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \overrightarrow{B C}{/tex} {tex}[\because \overrightarrow{A B}=-\overrightarrow{C D}]{/tex}

  3. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, {tex}\overrightarrow {AC}{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec a{/tex} + 2{tex}\vec b{/tex} ...(i)

    and in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD, 2{tex}\vec b{/tex} = 2{tex}\vec a{/tex} + {tex}\overrightarrow {BD}{/tex} ...(ii) [By triangle law of addition]
    Adding (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\overrightarrow {A C}+2 \vec{b}=4 \vec{a}+\overrightarrow {B D}+2 \vec{b}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{A C}-\overrightarrow{B D}=4 \vec{a}{/tex}
    OR
    Since T is the mid point of YZ
    So, {tex}\overrightarrow {YT} = \overrightarrow {TZ}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{X Y}+\overrightarrow{X Z}=(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y})+(\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Z}){/tex} [By triangle law]
    = 2{tex}\overrightarrow{X T}+\overrightarrow{T Y}+\overrightarrow{T Z}=2 \overrightarrow{X T}{/tex} [{tex}\because \overrightarrow{TY} = -\overrightarrow {YT}{/tex}]

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Sonu left from his village on weekend. First, he travelled d1 displacement up to a temple. After this, he left for the zoo and travelled d2 displacement. After this he left for shopping in a mall - Total driving time of Deepal from village to Mall was 1.5 hr.
If d1 = (6, 8) d2 = (3, 4) and d3 = (7, 12) km

  1. What is the total displacement from village to Mall? (1)
  2. What is the speed of Sonu from Village to Mall? (1)
  3. What is the Displacement from Village to Zoo? (2)
    OR
    What is the displacement from temple to Mall? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Sonu left from his village on weekend. First, he travelled d 1 displacement up to a temple. After this, he left for the zoo and travelled d 2 displacement. After this he left for shopping in a mall - Total driving time of Deepal from village to Mall was 1.5 hr. If d 1 = (6, 8) d 2 = (3, 4) and d 3 = (7, 12) km What is the total displacement from village to Mall? (1) What is the speed of Sonu from Village to Mall? (1) What is the Displacement from Village to Zoo? (2) OR What is the displacement from temple to Mall? (2)
explanation: Total displacement = {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert+\vert\vec{d_2}\vert+\vert\vec{d_3}\vert{/tex} {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert=\sqrt{6^2+8^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36+64}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex} = 10 km {tex}\vert\vec{d_2}\vert=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 km {tex}\vert\vec{d_3}\vert=\sqrt{7^2+12^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49+144}{/tex} = 13.89 Total displacement = 10 + 5 + 13.89 = 28.89 {tex}\approx{/tex} 29 km Speed = {tex}=\;{\textstyle\frac{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{distance}}{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{time}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{28.89}{1.5}{/tex} = 19.26 km/hr Displacement form village to zoo = d 1 + d 2 = 10 + 5 = 15 km OR Displacement from temple to mall = d 2 + d 3 = 5 + 13.89 = 18.89 {tex}\approx{/tex} 19 km
solution: Total displacement = {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert+\vert\vec{d_2}\vert+\vert\vec{d_3}\vert{/tex} {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert=\sqrt{6^2+8^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{36+64}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex} = 10 km {tex}\vert\vec{d_2}\vert=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+16}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5 km {tex}\vert\vec{d_3}\vert=\sqrt{7^2+12^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49+144}{/tex} = 13.89 Total displacement = 10 + 5 + 13.89 = 28.89 {tex}\approx{/tex} 29 km Speed = {tex}=\;{\textstyle\frac{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{distance}}{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{time}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{28.89}{1.5}{/tex} = 19.26 km/hr Displacement form village to zoo = d 1 + d 2 = 10 + 5 = 15 km OR Displacement from temple to mall = d 2 + d 3 = 5 + 13.89 = 18.89 {tex}\approx{/tex} 19 km
solution_html:
1. Total displacement = {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert+\vert\vec{d_2}\vert+\vert\vec{d_3}\vert{/tex}
    {tex}\vert\vec{d_1}\vert=\sqrt{6^2+8^2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{36+64}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
    = 10 km
    {tex}\vert\vec{d_2}\vert=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{9+16}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex}
    = 5 km
    {tex}\vert\vec{d_3}\vert=\sqrt{7^2+12^2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{49+144}{/tex}
    = 13.89
    Total displacement = 10 + 5 + 13.89
    = 28.89
    {tex}\approx{/tex} 29 km

  2. Speed = {tex}=\;{\textstyle\frac{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{distance}}{\mathrm{Total}\;\mathrm{time}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{28.89}{1.5}{/tex}
    = 19.26 km/hr

  3. Displacement form village to zoo = d1 + d2
    = 10 + 5
    = 15 km 
    OR
    Displacement from temple to mall = d2 + d3
    = 5 + 13.89
    = 18.89
    {tex}\approx{/tex} 19 km

question_html:
An instructor at the astronomical centre shows three among the brightest stars in a particular constellation. Assume that the telescope is located at {tex}{O}(0,0,0){/tex} and the three stars have their locations at the points {tex}{D}, {A}{/tex} and V having position vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, 7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. How far is the star V from star A? (1)
  2. Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the measure of {tex}\angle {VDA}{/tex}. (2)

      OR

    2. What is the projection of vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DV}}{/tex} on vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} ? (2)
question_text: An instructor at the astronomical centre shows three among the brightest stars in a particular constellation. Assume that the telescope is located at {tex}{O}(0,0,0){/tex} and the three stars have their locations at the points {tex}{D}, {A}{/tex} and V having position vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, 7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively. Based on the above information, answer the following questions: How far is the star V from star A? (1) Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} . (1) Find the measure of {tex}\angle {VDA}{/tex} . (2) OR What is the projection of vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DV}}{/tex} on vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{DA}}{/tex} ? (2)
explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex} OR {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex} OR {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\overrightarrow{A V}={/tex} Position Vector of V - Position Vector of A
    {tex}=-3 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+11 \hat{k}-7 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=-10 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Thus, {tex}|\overrightarrow{A V}|=\sqrt{100+4+9}=\sqrt{113}{/tex} units
  2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}={/tex} Position Vector of {tex}{A}\ -{/tex} Position Vector of D
    {tex}=7 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}=5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
    Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\frac{5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\angle V D A=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D V}||\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11 \sqrt{2}}{90}\right){/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}=-5 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      Projection of {tex}\overrightarrow{D V}{/tex} on {tex}\overrightarrow{D A}=\left(\frac{\overrightarrow{D V} \cdot \overrightarrow{D A}}{|\overrightarrow{D A}|}\right)=\frac{11 \sqrt{5}}{15}{/tex}

Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence, find the unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }.{/tex}
question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} is equal to one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and hence, find the unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}= ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | = \sqrt { ( 2 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 36 + 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} The unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } }{/tex} ...(i) According to the question, the scalar product of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} with unit vector {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} is 1. {tex}\therefore \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex} The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1. Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}

2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} [squaring both sides] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex} The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1. Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b } = { 2 } \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { c } = \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }.{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \lambda \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | = \sqrt { ( 2 + \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 4 + \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 36 + 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex}
The unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } }{/tex} ...(i)
According to the question, the scalar product of {tex}( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}with unit vector {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex} is 1.
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { \vec { b } + \vec { c } } { | \vec { b } + \vec { c } | } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \hat { k } ) \cdot \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 ( 2 + \lambda ) + 1 ( 6 ) + 1 ( - 2 ) } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { ( 2 + \lambda ) + 6 - 2 } { \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 } } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 6 = \sqrt { \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( \lambda + 6 ) ^ { 2 } = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex}
[squaring both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda ^ { 2 } + 36 + 12 \lambda = \lambda ^ { 2 } + 4 \lambda + 44{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 8 \lambda = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 1{/tex}
The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 1.
Substituting the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Eq. (i), we get unit vector along {tex}\vec { b } + \vec { c }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { ( 2 + 1 ) \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 4 ( 1 ) + 44 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 1 + 4 + 44 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { 3 } { 7 } \hat { i } + \frac { 6 } { 7 } \hat { j } - \frac { 2 } { 7 } \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html: Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex}and {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex}Find a vector {tex}\vec { p },{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18.{/tex}
question_text: Let {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } , \vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }.{/tex} Find a vector {tex}\vec { p },{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question vectors are {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Suppose, {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex} {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii) Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii) Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii), we get {tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex} Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get {tex}-9y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex} On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x = 64{/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution: According to the question vectors are {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex} {tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Suppose, {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex} {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii) Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii) Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii), we get {tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex} Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get {tex}-9y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex} On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} x = 64{/tex} Hence, the required vector is {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question vectors are

 {tex}\vec { a } = \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k },{/tex}
{tex}\vec { b } = 3 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
 {tex}\vec { c } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Suppose,  {tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
We have, {tex}\vec { p }{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec { a }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b }.{/tex}
{tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { a } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 4 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x + 4 y + 2 z = 0{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\vec { p }. \vec { b } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( x \hat { i } + { y } \hat {j } + z \hat { k } ) \cdot ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 x - 2 y + 7 z = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, given {tex}\vec { p } \cdot \vec { c } = 18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left({ x } \hat { i } + { y } \hat{ j } + z \hat { k } \right) \cdot ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 x - y + 4 z = 18{/tex} ...(iii)
Multiplying Eq. (i) by 3 and subtracting it from Eq. (ii),  we get
{tex}-14y + z = 0{/tex}
Multiplying Eq. (i) by 2 and subtracting it from Eq. (iii), we get
{tex}-9y = 18{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = -2{/tex}
On putting y = -2 and z= -28 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}x + 4 (-2) + 2 (-28) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} x - 8 - 56 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} x = 64{/tex}
Hence, the required vector is
{tex}\vec { p } = x \hat { i } + { y } \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec { p } = 64 \hat { i } - 2 \hat { j } - 28 \hat { k }{/tex}

question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex}, where {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k, \ \vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}.
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex} , where {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k, \ \vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec a+\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)+(\vec i+2\vec j+3\vec k)=2\vec i+3\vec j+4\vec k{/tex} {tex}\vec a-\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)-(\vec i+2\vec j+3 \vec k=-\vec j-2\vec k{/tex} A vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}(\vec a + \vec b){/tex} are {tex}(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} is given by {tex}(\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&4 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec c = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {24} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt 6 {/tex} Required unit vector is {tex}\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a+\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)+(\vec i+2\vec j+3\vec k)=2\vec i+3\vec j+4\vec k{/tex} {tex}\vec a-\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)-(\vec i+2\vec j+3 \vec k=-\vec j-2\vec k{/tex} A vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}(\vec a + \vec b){/tex} are {tex}(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} is given by {tex}(\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&4 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec c = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {24} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt 6 {/tex} Required unit vector is {tex}\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a+\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)+(\vec i+2\vec j+3\vec k)=2\vec i+3\vec j+4\vec k{/tex}
{tex}\vec a-\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)-(\vec i+2\vec j+3 \vec k=-\vec j-2\vec k{/tex}
A vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}(\vec a + \vec b){/tex} are {tex}(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} is given by
{tex}(\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&4 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec c = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {24} {/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt 6 {/tex}
Required unit vector is
{tex}\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}

question_html: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually {tex} \bot {/tex} unit vectors {tex}\hat i, \ \hat j, \ \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j,\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex}, where {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is {tex} \bot {/tex} to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex}
question_text: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually {tex} \bot {/tex} unit vectors {tex}\hat i, \ \hat j, \ \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j,\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex} , where {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is {tex} \bot {/tex} to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex} {tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex} {tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex} {tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex} {tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex} {tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex} {tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec \beta = \lambda \vec \alpha {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_1}\parallel to\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda (3\hat i - \hat j){/tex}
{tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex}
{tex} = (2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k) - (3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j){/tex}
{tex} = (2 - 3\lambda )i + (1 + \lambda )\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}3(2 - 3\lambda ) - (1 + \lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}

question_html: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is equal the one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}.
question_text: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k,{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is equal the one. Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex} According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex} {tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex} Squaring on both sides, {tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Unit vector along {tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex} According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex} {tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex} Squaring on both sides, {tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec b = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec a + \vec b = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
Unit vector along
{tex}\vec a + \vec b = \frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a + \vec b} \right|}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }}{/tex}
According to question, {tex}\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + 6\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40}}} \right) = 1{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + 6 - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} }} = 1{/tex}
{tex}2 + \lambda + 4 = \sqrt {{{\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)}^2} + 40} {/tex}
Squaring on both sides,
{tex}{\lambda ^2} + 36 + 12\lambda = {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)^2} + 40{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
