question_html: Show that the points A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2) and C (11, 3, 7) are collinear, and find the ratio in which B divides AC.
question_text: Show that the points A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2) and C (11, 3, 7) are collinear, and find the ratio in which B divides AC.
explanation: A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2), C (11, 3, 7) {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = 1\hat i - 2\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 5\hat i - 0\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 11\hat i + 3\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} {/tex} {tex} = \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = 3\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \frac{3}{2}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} Thus {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} ||\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and one point B is common therefore A, B, C are collinear and B divides AC in 2:3.
solution: A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2), C (11, 3, 7) {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = 1\hat i - 2\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 5\hat i - 0\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 11\hat i + 3\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} {/tex} {tex} = \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = 3\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \frac{3}{2}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} Thus {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} ||\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and one point B is common therefore A, B, C are collinear and B divides AC in 2:3.
solution_html:
A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2), C (11, 3, 7)

{tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = 1\hat i - 2\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 5\hat i - 0\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 11\hat i + 3\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex}

{tex}= 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} {/tex}

{tex} = \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 3\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \frac{3}{2}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex}

Thus {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} ||\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex}  and one point B is common therefore A, B, C are collinear and B divides AC in 2:3.

question_html: If {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d },{/tex}then show that {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c }.{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d },{/tex} then show that {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c },{/tex} where {tex}\vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c }.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), {tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given] The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), {tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given] The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { c } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\vec { a } \times \vec { c } = \vec { b } \times \vec { d }{/tex} ...(ii)
Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), 
{tex}( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) = ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ( \vec { a } \times \vec { b } ) - ( \vec { a } \times \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } \times \vec { d } ) - ( \vec { c } \times \vec { d } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) + ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) \times \vec { d } = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { a } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) - \vec { d } \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = - \vec { b } \times \vec { a } ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \vec { a } - \vec { d } ) \times ( \vec { b } - \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \vec { a } \neq \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } \neq \vec { c },{/tex} given]
The cross-product of vectors {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }{/tex} is a zero vector, so {tex}\vec { a } - \vec { d }{/tex} is parallel {tex}\vec { b } - \vec { c }.{/tex}

question_html: Find the position vector of a point R, which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are 2{tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} respectively, externally in the ratio 1: 2. Also, show that P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
question_text: Find the position vector of a point R, which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are 2 {tex}\vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} respectively, externally in the ratio 1: 2. Also, show that P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} Suppose {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex} [using external section formula] {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ, i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex} P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
solution: According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex} = Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} Suppose {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex} [using external section formula] {tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex} We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ, i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex} {tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex} P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}\vec { O P }{/tex}= Position vector of {tex}P = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { O Q }{/tex}= Position vector of {tex}Q = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex}
Suppose  {tex}\vec { O R }{/tex} be the position vector of point R, which divides {tex}PQ\ in\ the\ ratio\ 1: 2{/tex} externally

{tex}\therefore \quad \vec { O R } = \frac { 1 ( \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } ) - 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 1 - 2 }{/tex}
[using external section formula]
{tex}= \frac { \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b } - 4 \vec { a } - 2 \vec { b } } { - 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 3 \vec { a } - 5 \vec { b } } { - 1 } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b }{/tex}
We have to show that P is the mid-point of RQ,
i.e. {tex}\vec { O P } = \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\vec { O R } = 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } , \vec { O Q } = \vec { a } - 3 \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { \vec { O R } + \vec { O Q } } { 2 } = \frac { ( 3 \vec { a } + 5 \vec { b } ) + \vec { ( a } - 3 \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 \vec { a } + 2 \vec { b } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 ( 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ) } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b }{/tex}
{tex}= \vec { O P }{/tex}  {tex}[ \because \vec { O P } = 2 \vec { a } + \vec { b } ]{/tex}
P is the mid-point of line segment RQ.

question_html: Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is {tex} \bot {/tex} to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}. {tex}\vec d = 15{/tex} Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k,\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
question_text: Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is {tex} \bot {/tex} to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} . {tex}\vec d = 15{/tex} Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k,\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k,\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec d = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} ATQ, {tex}\vec d.\vec a = 0,\vec d.\vec b = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} , then, x + 4y + 2z = 0 ...(1) 3x - 2y + 7z = 0 ...(2) 2x - y + 4z = 15 ...(3) On solving equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{x}{{28 + 4}} = \frac{y}{{6 - 7}} = \frac{z}{{ - 2 - 12}} = k{/tex} x = 32k, y = -k, z = -14k Put x, y, z in equation (3) 2(32k) - (-k) + 4(-14k) = 15 64k + k - 56k = 15 9k = 15 {tex}k = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}x = 32 \times \frac{5}{3} = \frac{{160}}{3}{/tex} {tex}y = - \frac{5}{3} {/tex} {tex}z = - 14 \times \frac{5}{3} = - \frac{{70}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\vec d = \frac{{160}}{3}\hat i - \frac{5}{3}\hat j - \frac{{70}}{3}\hat k{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k,\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec d = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} ATQ, {tex}\vec d.\vec a = 0,\vec d.\vec b = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} , then, x + 4y + 2z = 0 ...(1) 3x - 2y + 7z = 0 ...(2) 2x - y + 4z = 15 ...(3) On solving equation (1) and (2) {tex}\frac{x}{{28 + 4}} = \frac{y}{{6 - 7}} = \frac{z}{{ - 2 - 12}} = k{/tex} x = 32k, y = -k, z = -14k Put x, y, z in equation (3) 2(32k) - (-k) + 4(-14k) = 15 64k + k - 56k = 15 9k = 15 {tex}k = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} {tex}k = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}x = 32 \times \frac{5}{3} = \frac{{160}}{3}{/tex} {tex}y = - \frac{5}{3} {/tex} {tex}z = - 14 \times \frac{5}{3} = - \frac{{70}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\vec d = \frac{{160}}{3}\hat i - \frac{5}{3}\hat j - \frac{{70}}{3}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k,\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} 
Let {tex}\vec d = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} 
ATQ, {tex}\vec d.\vec a = 0,\vec d.\vec b = 0{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}, then,
x + 4y + 2z = 0 ...(1)
3x - 2y + 7z = 0 ...(2)
2x - y + 4z = 15 ...(3)
On solving equation (1) and (2)

{tex}\frac{x}{{28 + 4}} = \frac{y}{{6 - 7}} = \frac{z}{{ - 2 - 12}} = k{/tex} 
x = 32k, y = -k, z = -14k
Put x, y, z in equation (3)
2(32k) - (-k) + 4(-14k) = 15
64k + k - 56k = 15
9k = 15
{tex}k = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} 
{tex}k = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}x = 32 \times \frac{5}{3} = \frac{{160}}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}y = - \frac{5}{3} {/tex} 
{tex}z = - 14 \times \frac{5}{3} = - \frac{{70}}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec d = \frac{{160}}{3}\hat i - \frac{5}{3}\hat j - \frac{{70}}{3}\hat k{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex} so that the vectors {tex}2\hat i - 4\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}4\hat i - 8\hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex} are (i) parallel (ii) perpendicular.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex} so that the vectors {tex}2\hat i - 4\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}4\hat i - 8\hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex} are (i) parallel (ii) perpendicular.
explanation: Given vectors are parallel if {tex}\ (2\vec i-4\vec j +\vec k)×(4\vec i-8\vec j+\lambda\vec k)=0 {/tex} i.e if {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 4}&1 \\ 4&{ - 8}&\lambda \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec i(-4\lambda+8)-\vec j(2\lambda-4)+\vec k(-16+16)=\vec 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -4\lambda+8=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=2{/tex} For {tex} \bot {/tex} {tex}\left( {4\hat i - 8\hat j + \lambda \hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i - 4\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}8 + 32 + \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = - 40{/tex}
solution: Given vectors are parallel if {tex}\ (2\vec i-4\vec j +\vec k)×(4\vec i-8\vec j+\lambda\vec k)=0 {/tex} i.e if {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 4}&1 \\ 4&{ - 8}&\lambda \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec i(-4\lambda+8)-\vec j(2\lambda-4)+\vec k(-16+16)=\vec 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -4\lambda+8=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=2{/tex} For {tex} \bot {/tex} {tex}\left( {4\hat i - 8\hat j + \lambda \hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i - 4\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}8 + 32 + \lambda = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = - 40{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given vectors are parallel
    if {tex}\ (2\vec i-4\vec j +\vec k)×(4\vec i-8\vec j+\lambda\vec k)=0 {/tex}
    i.e if {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 4}&1 \\ 4&{ - 8}&\lambda \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\vec i(-4\lambda+8)-\vec j(2\lambda-4)+\vec k(-16+16)=\vec 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow -4\lambda+8=0{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=2{/tex}
  2. For {tex} \bot {/tex}
    {tex}\left( {4\hat i - 8\hat j + \lambda \hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i - 4\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0{/tex}
    {tex}8 + 32 + \lambda = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda = - 40{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are unit vector such that {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} find the value of {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are unit vector such that {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} find the value of {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 1{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} (Given) {tex}\vec a.\left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec a + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}1 + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(i) Similarly, {tex}\vec b.\vec a + \vec b.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(ii) again {tex}\vec c.\vec a + \vec c.\vec b = - 1{/tex} ...(iii) adding (i), (ii) and (iii) {tex}2\left( {\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a} \right) = - 3{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because{\vec a.\vec b = \vec b.\vec a} \right ]{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a = - \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 1{/tex} {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} (Given) {tex}\vec a.\left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec a + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}1 + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(i) Similarly, {tex}\vec b.\vec a + \vec b.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(ii) again {tex}\vec c.\vec a + \vec c.\vec b = - 1{/tex} ...(iii) adding (i), (ii) and (iii) {tex}2\left( {\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a} \right) = - 3{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because{\vec a.\vec b = \vec b.\vec a} \right ]{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a = - \frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 1,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 1{/tex}

{tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = 0{/tex} (Given)

{tex}\vec a.\left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\vec a.\vec a + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}1 + \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(i)

Similarly,

{tex}\vec b.\vec a + \vec b.\vec c = - 1{/tex} ...(ii)

again

{tex}\vec c.\vec a + \vec c.\vec b = - 1{/tex} ...(iii)

adding (i), (ii) and (iii)

{tex}2\left( {\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a} \right) = - 3{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because{\vec a.\vec b = \vec b.\vec a} \right ]{/tex}

{tex}\vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec c + \vec c.\vec a = - \frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k, \ \vec b = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} find (i) Magnitude of {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} (ii) A unit vector which is  {tex} \ \bot to\ both\ \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}  (iii) The cosine and sine of the angle b/w the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k, \ \vec b = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} find (i) Magnitude of {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} (ii) A unit vector which is {tex} \ \bot to\ both\ \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} (iii) The cosine and sine of the angle b/w the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 3&1&2 \\ 2&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 8\hat i - 8\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| =\sqrt{8^2+8^2+8^2}=\sqrt{64+64+64}=8\sqrt 3 {/tex} A unit vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex} \vec b{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\vec a \times \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}} = \frac{{8\hat i - 8\hat j - 8\hat k}}{{8\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b= (3\hat i+\hat j+2\hat k)(2\hat i-2\hat j+4\hat k)= 12{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| =\sqrt{9+1+4}= \sqrt {14} {/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right| =\sqrt{4+4+16}= \sqrt {24}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{12}}{{\left( {\sqrt {14} } \right)\left( {\sqrt {24} } \right)}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{8\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt {14} \sqrt {24} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 3&1&2 \\ 2&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 8\hat i - 8\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| =\sqrt{8^2+8^2+8^2}=\sqrt{64+64+64}=8\sqrt 3 {/tex} A unit vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex} \vec b{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\vec a \times \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}} = \frac{{8\hat i - 8\hat j - 8\hat k}}{{8\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\vec a.\vec b= (3\hat i+\hat j+2\hat k)(2\hat i-2\hat j+4\hat k)= 12{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| =\sqrt{9+1+4}= \sqrt {14} {/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right| =\sqrt{4+4+16}= \sqrt {24}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{12}}{{\left( {\sqrt {14} } \right)\left( {\sqrt {24} } \right)}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{8\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt {14} \sqrt {24} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 3&1&2 \\ 2&{ - 2}&4 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
    {tex} = 8\hat i - 8\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex}
    {tex}\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| =\sqrt{8^2+8^2+8^2}=\sqrt{64+64+64}=8\sqrt 3 {/tex}
  2. A unit vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex} \vec b{/tex} ={tex}\frac{{\vec a \times \vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}} = \frac{{8\hat i - 8\hat j - 8\hat k}}{{8\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\vec a.\vec b= (3\hat i+\hat j+2\hat k)(2\hat i-2\hat j+4\hat k)= 12{/tex}
    {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| =\sqrt{9+1+4}= \sqrt {14} {/tex}
    {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right| =\sqrt{4+4+16}= \sqrt {24}{/tex}
    {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{12}}{{\left( {\sqrt {14} } \right)\left( {\sqrt {24} } \right)}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}
    {tex}\sin \theta = \frac{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{8\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt {14} \sqrt {24} }}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}

question_html: Write a vector of magnitude 12 units which makes 45° angle with X-axis, 60° angle with Y-axis and an obtuse angle with Z-axis.
question_text: Write a vector of magnitude 12 units which makes 45° angle with X-axis, 60° angle with Y-axis and an obtuse angle with Z-axis.
explanation: Suppose that the required vector {tex}=\vec{r}{/tex} If {tex}\alpha, \beta, \gamma{/tex} are angle that {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} makes with the coordinate axes x, y and z respectively then {tex}l=\cos \alpha{/tex} {tex}=\cos 45^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}l=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}m=\cos \beta{/tex} {tex}=\cos 60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex} We know that, {tex}l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{2} r\right)=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\cos ^{2} \gamma=1-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4-2-1}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Neglecting since {tex}\gamma{/tex} is an obtuse angle. {tex}\cos \gamma=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}n=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\text { vector } \vec{r}=\vec{r}(\{l\hat{i}+m \hat{j}+n \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=12\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}-\frac{1}{2} n \hat{k}\right){/tex} {tex}=12\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Suppose that the required vector {tex}=\vec{r}{/tex} If {tex}\alpha, \beta, \gamma{/tex} are angle that {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} makes with the coordinate axes x, y and z respectively then {tex}l=\cos \alpha{/tex} {tex}=\cos 45^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}l=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}m=\cos \beta{/tex} {tex}=\cos 60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}m=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex} We know that, {tex}l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{2} r\right)=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\cos ^{2} \gamma=1-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4-2-1}{4}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cos \gamma=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} Neglecting since {tex}\gamma{/tex} is an obtuse angle. {tex}\cos \gamma=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\gamma=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}n=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\text { vector } \vec{r}=\vec{r}(\{l\hat{i}+m \hat{j}+n \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=12\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}-\frac{1}{2} n \hat{k}\right){/tex} {tex}=12\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose that the required vector {tex}=\vec{r}{/tex}
If {tex}\alpha, \beta, \gamma{/tex}  are angle that {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} makes with the coordinate axes x, y and z  respectively then
{tex}l=\cos \alpha{/tex}
{tex}=\cos 45^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}l=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}m=\cos \beta{/tex}
{tex}=\cos 60^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}m=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex}
We know that,
{tex}l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{2} r\right)=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex}
{tex}\cos ^{2} \gamma=1-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4-2-1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \gamma=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \gamma=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
 Neglecting since {tex}\gamma{/tex} is an obtuse angle.
{tex}\cos \gamma=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\gamma=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}n=\cos \gamma{/tex}
{tex}=\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\cos \left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=-\cos \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}n=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Therefore,
{tex}\text { vector } \vec{r}=\vec{r}(\{l\hat{i}+m \hat{j}+n \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=12\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{i}+\frac{1}{2} \hat{j}-\frac{1}{2} n \hat{k}\right){/tex}
{tex}=12\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=6(\sqrt{2} i+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Find the area of the {tex}\Delta{/tex} with vertices A (1, 1, 2) B (2, 3, 5) and C (1, 5, 5).
question_text: Find the area of the {tex}\Delta{/tex} with vertices A (1, 1, 2) B (2, 3, 5) and C (1, 5, 5).
explanation: A (1, 1, 2) B(2, 3, 5) C (1, 5, 5) {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i + 5\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OA} = 4\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 0&4&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 6\hat i - 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2} {\sqrt {{{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2} + {4^2}} } {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {61} {/tex} sq. unit
solution: A (1, 1, 2) B(2, 3, 5) C (1, 5, 5) {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i + 5\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OA} = 4\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 0&4&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 6\hat i - 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{2} {\sqrt {{{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2} + {4^2}} } {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {61} {/tex} sq. unit
solution_html:
A (1, 1, 2) B(2, 3, 5) C (1, 5, 5)

{tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i + 5\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OA} = 4\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 0&4&3 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}

{tex} = - 6\hat i - 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}

Area of {tex}\Delta ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2} {\sqrt {{{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2} + {4^2}} } {/tex}

{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {61} {/tex} sq. unit

question_text: A vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} has magnitude 14 units and direction ratios 2, 3, -6. Find the direction cosines and components of {tex}\vec r{/tex} , given that {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with x-axis.
explanation: Here, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 14,\vec a = 2k,\vec b = 3k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = - 6k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines l, m and n are {tex}l = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{2k}}{{14}} = \frac{k}{7}{/tex} {tex}m = \frac{{\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{3k}}{{14}}{/tex} And {tex}n = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 6k}}{{14}} = \frac{{ - 3k}}{7}{/tex} Also, we know that {tex}{l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{{49}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{196}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{49}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{4{k^2} + 9{k^2} + 36{k^2}}}{{196}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{196}}{{49}} = 4{/tex} {tex} {k} = \pm 2{/tex} So, the direction cosines (l, m, n) are {tex}\frac{2}{7},\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{ - 6}}{7}{/tex} [since, {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with X-axis] {tex}Now, \vec r = \hat r.\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right)\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left( {\frac{{ 2}}{7}\hat i + \frac{3}{7}\hat j - \frac{6}{7}\hat k} \right).14{/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k{/tex}
solution: Here, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 14,\vec a = 2k,\vec b = 3k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = - 6k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines l, m and n are {tex}l = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{2k}}{{14}} = \frac{k}{7}{/tex} {tex}m = \frac{{\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{3k}}{{14}}{/tex} And {tex}n = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 6k}}{{14}} = \frac{{ - 3k}}{7}{/tex} Also, we know that {tex}{l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{{49}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{196}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{49}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{4{k^2} + 9{k^2} + 36{k^2}}}{{196}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{196}}{{49}} = 4{/tex} {tex} {k} = \pm 2{/tex} So, the direction cosines (l, m, n) are {tex}\frac{2}{7},\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{ - 6}}{7}{/tex} [since, {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with X-axis] {tex}Now, \vec r = \hat r.\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right)\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left( {\frac{{ 2}}{7}\hat i + \frac{3}{7}\hat j - \frac{6}{7}\hat k} \right).14{/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 14,\vec a = 2k,\vec b = 3k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = - 6k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines l, m and n are
{tex}l = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{2k}}{{14}} = \frac{k}{7}{/tex}
{tex}m = \frac{{\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{3k}}{{14}}{/tex}
And {tex}n = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 6k}}{{14}} = \frac{{ - 3k}}{7}{/tex}
Also, we know that
{tex}{l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{{49}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{196}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{49}} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{4{k^2} + 9{k^2} + 36{k^2}}}{{196}} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{196}}{{49}} = 4{/tex}
{tex} {k} = \pm 2{/tex}
So, the direction cosines (l, m, n) are {tex}\frac{2}{7},\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{ - 6}}{7}{/tex}
[since, {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with X-axis]
{tex}Now, \vec r = \hat r.\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right)\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \left( {\frac{{ 2}}{7}\hat i + \frac{3}{7}\hat j - \frac{6}{7}\hat k} \right).14{/tex}
{tex} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k{/tex}
